skill.name: using-git-worktrees
skill.description: Create isolated git worktrees with directory selection, branch setup, safety checks, cleanup steps, and troubleshooting guidance.
skill.summary: Guides agents through safe Git worktree setup for isolated parallel development.
skill.author: Doyajin174

security_audit.summary: The static Ruby backtick, path traversal, weak crypto, device file, and reconnaissance alerts are false positives caused by Markdown examples and normal Git documentation. The skill is still medium risk because it instructs agents to run Git, dependency installation, editor, and cleanup commands that can change local files.

medium_findings.title: Local Command Execution Guidance
medium_findings.description: The skill provides workflow examples that run local Git, shell, dependency installation, and editor commands. This is expected for a Git worktree skill, but agents should confirm repository state and user intent before executing commands that alter branches, install packages, or open tools.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file clearly contains executable local command examples. The purpose is legitimate Git worktree management, so this is a controlled operational risk rather than malicious behavior.

medium_findings.title: Recursive Directory Deletion Example
medium_findings.description: The cleanup workflow includes recursive deletion of an old worktree directory. The example targets a relative worktree folder, but an agent or user could cause unintended data loss if the path is changed or resolved from the wrong directory.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The recursive deletion command is explicit and can remove files. The scoped example path reduces malicious concern but still warrants a medium warning.

medium_findings.title: Dependency Installation Can Execute Project Scripts
medium_findings.description: The feature workflow suggests installing dependencies inside a new worktree. This can be necessary, but package installation may run lifecycle scripts from the checked-out project.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The dependency installation guidance is directly present. The risk depends on the target project contents, so the finding is moderate rather than high.

low_findings.title: Markdown Fence Misclassified as Ruby Backticks
low_findings.description: The static analyzer reported Ruby or shell backtick execution across Markdown code fences and command examples. These are documentation blocks, not executable Ruby interpolation or hidden runtime code.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The suspicious tokens are Markdown fences or inline Markdown formatting. No Ruby file, dynamic evaluation, or command substitution is present.

low_findings.title: Relative Worktree Paths Are Benign Documentation
low_findings.description: The repeated parent-directory paths are used to place Git worktrees beside the main repository. They do not access sensitive system paths, read secrets, or escape a sandbox by themselves.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Every path traversal alert points to the documented worktree directory convention. The only material risk is operational misuse, which is covered by the medium cleanup finding.

low_findings.title: Device File Suppression Is Low Risk
low_findings.description: The device file reference redirects command errors to the null device while checking for a worktree directory. It does not read or write sensitive device data.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The device file appears only in an error-suppression redirect. This is a standard shell idiom with no exfiltration or privilege behavior.

low_findings.title: Weak Crypto Alert Is a False Positive
low_findings.description: The static analyzer flagged the description line for weak cryptography, but the skill contains no cryptographic algorithm, hashing operation, encryption code, or credential handling.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Line 3 is plain metadata describing isolated Git worktrees. No crypto-related code or instruction exists in the file.

low_findings.title: System Reconnaissance Alert Is a False Positive
low_findings.description: The troubleshooting note recommends fetching Git refs when a ref is missing. This is normal repository maintenance, not host reconnaissance or discovery of sensitive system data.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The referenced line concerns Git ref troubleshooting only. It does not enumerate system users, processes, networks, or host configuration.

dangerous_patterns.title: Recursive Removal Command
dangerous_patterns.description: The documentation includes a recursive removal command for unused worktree directories. This should be executed only after confirming the resolved path.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The command is directly present and destructive if pointed at the wrong path. The documented target is a worktree cleanup folder.

dangerous_patterns.title: Package Installation Command
dangerous_patterns.description: The documentation suggests dependency installation in a worktree. Package managers can execute project-defined install scripts.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The package install command is directly present. The actual risk depends on the project being checked out in the worktree.

content.value_statement: Parallel Git work can become risky when branches share one working directory. This skill guides Claude, Codex, and Claude Code through isolated worktree setup, checks, cleanup, and troubleshooting.
actual_capabilities: Selects a consistent sibling directory pattern for Git worktrees.
Creates feature, hotfix, experiment, and review worktrees from branches or refs.
Checks repository status, branch availability, stashes, remotes, and active worktrees.
Explains cleanup steps for removing finished worktrees and pruning stale references.
Provides troubleshooting guidance for checked-out branches, missing refs, and dependency issues.
limitations: Requires an existing Git repository and a working Git command-line environment.
Does not replace branch review, test execution, or merge conflict resolution.
Dependency installation guidance depends on each project package manager.
Cleanup commands require careful path confirmation before execution.

use_cases.title: Start Feature Development
use_cases.description: Create a separate worktree from the main branch before building a new feature.

use_cases.title: Review Pull Requests
use_cases.description: Check out a review branch in a separate directory without disrupting active work.

use_cases.title: Handle Urgent Hotfixes
use_cases.description: Open a hotfix worktree while preserving unfinished local changes in the main workspace.

prompt_templates.title: Create My First Worktree
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the using-git-worktrees skill to help me create a feature worktree for this repository. Check my current Git status first and explain each step before running commands.

prompt_templates.title: Set Up a Review Branch
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the using-git-worktrees skill to create a review worktree for a remote pull request branch. Verify the ref exists and keep the new directory separate from my current work.

prompt_templates.title: Prepare a Hotfix Worktree
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the using-git-worktrees skill to prepare a hotfix worktree from the latest main branch. Confirm my current changes are safe before creating the branch.

prompt_templates.title: Audit and Clean Worktrees
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the using-git-worktrees skill to review all worktrees, identify stale entries, and propose a cleanup plan. Ask before deleting any directory or branch.

output_examples.input: Create a feature worktree for a new authentication task.
output_examples.output: The assistant checks repository status, confirms the base branch, creates a sibling worktree, enters the new directory, and verifies the active branch.

output_examples.input: Review a remote branch without changing my current workspace.
output_examples.output: The assistant fetches refs, validates the remote branch, creates a review worktree, and lists follow-up checks for tests and code review.
The assistant lists active worktrees, identifies stale directories, asks for confirmation, removes selected worktrees, and prunes stale Git metadata.

best_practices: Confirm current Git status before creating or deleting a worktree.
Use clear directory and branch names that describe the task.
Review resolved paths before running cleanup commands.
anti_patterns: Deleting worktree directories without checking the path first.
Creating multiple worktrees with unclear branch names.
Installing dependencies in untrusted branches without reviewing project scripts.

faq.question: What does this skill help with?
faq.answer: It helps create, verify, use, and clean Git worktrees for isolated development.

faq.question: Does it make changes to my repository?
faq.answer: It can guide commands that create branches, add worktrees, install dependencies, and remove worktrees.

faq.question: Is it safe to use with active local changes?
faq.answer: It includes status checks, but you should commit or stash important changes before risky operations.

faq.question: Can it review pull request branches?
faq.answer: Yes. It can guide setup for a separate review worktree from a remote branch or ref.

faq.question: Does it manage dependencies automatically?
faq.answer: It suggests installing dependencies when needed, but the correct command depends on the project.

faq.question: Why is the risk level medium?
faq.answer: The skill is legitimate, but it includes commands that can modify files, install packages, or delete directories.
